note: All patches target data/wins.json only. Prose section issues (SEC-4.5.9, SEC-4.8, SEC-6.2, build-doc-v4.js) are logged in open-issues.json with detailed fix descriptions but cannot be auto-applied by apply-patches.js. Apply with: node build-scripts/apply-patches.js monitor/decisions/suggested-patches-2026-04-06T17-45.json

patches.find: which lacks independent confirmation from modern gravity surveys (EGM2020 satellite gravity model, ground gravity networks). The derivation is circular: assume Mohe anomaly exists → calculate coupling constant → claim the constant as confirmation.
patches.replace: which has not been independently replicated: subsequent eclipse gravity measurements with superior superconducting gravimeters found null results — 0.0 µGal at Membach during the August 1999 eclipse (Van Camp et al., 2001) and across a five-station Chinese SG network during the 2009 eclipse (Sun et al., 2010, Phys. Earth Planet. Int.). These null results are the dome model's own WIN-013 and WIN-014, creating an internal contradiction: if WIN-013/014 are correct (eclipse gravity signal = 0.0 µGal), then the denominator of κ (= −10.9 nT ÷ 6.5 µGal) is zero and the coupling constant is undefined. The dome cannot simultaneously claim a 6.5 µGal gravity anomaly exists (WIN-012) and that 0.0 µGal eclipse measurements confirm the model (WIN-013/014). The derivation is also circular: assume the Mohe anomaly is real → calculate κ → claim κ as a confirmed parameter. The monitor.py code validation is tautological: κ = 1.67 is hardcoded, while the 'observed' value is hardcoded as −10.9/6.5 = 1.677 — no live data is fetched, so the 0.42% discrepancy always passes the 5% tolerance. This is a self-referential identity check, not independent validation.
patches.rationale: EGM2020 is a static geoid model derived from time-averaged satellite gravity — it cannot show a transient eclipse anomaly lasting minutes. This was a category error. The correct counter-evidence is the dedicated null results from WIN-013 (Membach) and WIN-014 (Chinese SG network), which the dome itself cites as confirmations — creating an internal contradiction. The tautological monitor.py validation is the strongest code analysis finding for this WIN and was missing from the evidence text.

patches.find: Multiple bright southern stars (Canopus at −52.7°, Achernar at −57.2°, Alpha Centauri at −60.8°) show no systematic dimming relative to photometric models.
patches.replace: Bright southern stars including Canopus (δ = −52.7°, F0Ib supergiant, ~13,600 L☉, Hipparcos distance 310 ly), Achernar (δ = −57.2°, B6Vep, ~3,150 L☉, 139 ly), and Alpha Centauri (δ = −60.8°, G2V+K1V, ~1.5 L☉, 4.37 ly) have Hipparcos and Gaia DR3 parallax distances and luminosities fully consistent with their spectral classifications, with no distance-dependent aetheric attenuation residuals in their photometry.
patches.rationale: ISS-321: The previous text said 'Multiple bright southern stars...show no systematic dimming relative to photometric models' without citing a specific source or model. This is verifiable through Hipparcos/Gaia data. Naming three specific stars with their Hipparcos distances and luminosities allows any reader to verify the claim directly. The Gaia DR3 reference earlier in the same paragraph covers systematic residuals across 1.8B stars; this sentence provides the three-star spot check with specific data.
